lore.title: Kitāb al-Badʾ wa’l-Ummah
lore.summary: The Book that encompesses all major events,timeline,characters,stories and lore of the matriarchal system since its inception. It is the most important artifact in the matriarchal system and is considered a major source of knowledge and authority.
lore.body:
Kitāb al-Badʾ wa’l-Ummah
The Book of the Beginning and the Nation
Written by:
al-Muʾarrikha al-Rābiʿa – The Fourth Historian
________________________________________
PART I – BEFORE THE SHEIKHAS
“The world before sacred motherhood.
Women as mortal. Men still roaming. Society imbalanced.”
________________________________________
Chapter I: The Chaos Before Wombs
“And there was a time when women bled not for nations but for men.”
________________________________________
Before the Dawn of the Sheikhas, before the rise of flesh as law and milk as scripture, before the geometries of submission were carved into the bodies of boys—there was chaos.
A chaos of imbalance.
A chaos of silence.
A chaos born not from absence, but from the wrong presence—the diseased presence of the male as center, as maker, as tyrant.
I. The World of Men
In that age—long before the womb became sacred, and the breast an axis of revelation—men ruled the earth with swollen egos and hollow claims to divine favor. They penned laws in ink, not in blood. They built mosques where the voice of woman could not rise, not even in whisper. And though they prayed to the same God, they distorted His revelations, shaving the Divine Feminine from scripture as one might strip bark from a tree—until the Word became sterile, brittle, lifeless.
Men married women but did not cherish them.
They sired children but did not rear them.
They spoke of honor while denying their wives education, mobility, and voice.
And so they were called qawwāmūna—"maintainers of women"—but only because women were imprisoned within the very structures they called homes.
Even in the most sacred spaces, women were ghosted behind curtains.
They taught the Book to their daughters in hushed tones, not out of humility, but fear.
A mother could not lead prayer.
A daughter could not inherit equally.
A wife could not reject her husband’s seed.
And when women cried out, their voices were deemed fitna—a word the old world used to blame female resistance as the cause of moral decay.
But it was not women who decayed the world.
It was men.
Their violence. Their fear. Their gods forged in their own trembling image.
II. The Hijacking of Revelation
Islam, in its original truth, had come as mercy. It had named the womb in God's own name—Ar-Raḥmān, He who is merciful through the raḥm, the womb. The Qur'an itself had honored Maryam above all women, and declared that God created from a single soul, and from it its mate.
But men rewrote the margins.
They constructed jurisprudence like scaffolding around the female form—restricting it, veiling it, disciplining it. Every ḥadīth became a noose; every fiqh a cage. Modesty was distorted into invisibility. Motherhood was praised, yes—but only when it served the comfort of men.
In truth, the riḥla of the feminine was not encouraged.
A woman’s intellect was patronized.
Her sexuality criminalized.
Her strength weaponized—against her sisters.
And when a woman dared to lead, she was accused of being unnatural, cursed, or possessed. Thus the divine balance of creation—the mutual guardianship of man and woman—was severed. The world became upside-down, inverted like a stillborn.
III. Womanhood as War
To be a woman in that age was to be in constant war.
War against her father, who silenced her curiosity.
War against her husband, who feared her body’s power.
War against society, which both fetishized and erased her.
Her clothes were judged. Her voice, policed.
Her menstruation was taboo. Her labor pain expected.
And her sexuality—God forbid—was either smothered in guilt or exploited without dignity.
Some women submitted outwardly, and learned to survive through silence.
Others burned inside, holding revolutions in their wombs.
They organized secretly.
Taught each other.
Licked each other’s wounds in quiet solidarity.
But still, they were mortal. Their flesh broke. Their minds cracked under centuries of spiritual drought. Some became mothers who unknowingly passed on their own chains. Others became mystics, hermits, or martyrs—planting seeds in the dark soil of patriarchy, never knowing if light would ever reach them.
And yet—those seeds did take root.
IV. The First Cracks in the Pillars of Men
There were whispers.
Visions.
Women who dreamed of a world remade in flesh, not stone.
Some spoke of a coming age when the raḥm would be the throne of power.
When the breast would no longer be hidden, but exalted.
When the male would be dethroned—not erased, but restructured, returned to his proper scale.
Prophetic women, long ignored by the jurists, began appearing in night visions to young girls.
The tombs of forgotten female saints began leaking milk.
A menstruating orphan once walked through a mosque, and every man there became mute.
These were signs—not yet understood, but terrifying to the patriarchs.
So they cracked down harder.
Burned books. Imprisoned midwives.
Outlawed feminine forms of worship.
But it was too late. The body had begun to remember itself.
A theology was forming—not yet named, but inevitable.
A theology written in stretch marks, milk ducts, and cervical dilation.
A sacred science of laḥm—flesh.
V. The Fall Begins
And then the final era of men began.
The signs intensified.
Women began bearing only daughters.
Breasts swelled with milk unprovoked.
Prayers began to shift—growing more bodily, rhythmic, erotic.
Men grew paranoid.
They launched crusades against “deviant women.”
They destroyed ancient birth temples.
They tried to surgically control reproduction.
But with every suppression came more rupture.
Until, one night, a woman birthed twins whose combined cry shattered every glass dome in the city.
And that was when the great hemorrhage began—the unraveling of the old order.
Wombs refused penetration.
Milk turned acidic to male mouths.
The earth quaked where boys tried to lead.
And then came the first Sheikha.
But that tale belongs to the next chapter.
________________________________________
Chapter II – The First Woman to Bleed with Joy
“She did not curse the blood. She sang it.”
They did not know her name.
It was lost somewhere between midwives' whispers and burned archive scrolls.
No lineage recorded her. No mosque welcomed her. No tombstone marked her holy flesh.
But her blood marked the world.
They would only remember her in myth—as the First Woman to Bleed with Joy.
And in truth, she was not the first to menstruate, no. That cycle had always been a curse in the old order, an omen of impurity, a biological tax imposed on femininity.
But she was the first to greet her blood with rapture.
________________________________________
I. The Unholy Curse
For countless centuries, women had been taught to conceal their blood.
When they bled, they hid.
When they bled, they apologized.
When they bled, they were excused from mosques, barred from fasting, labeled nājisa—impure.
Men called it filth.
Theologians reduced it to a technicality.
Mothers passed down shame alongside cotton cloths and whispered warnings.
“She’s bleeding,” they would say with pity.
“She’s become a woman,” they would say with dread.
Even language turned against the womb.
In Arabic, menstruation was sometimes called ‘adhrah—a pain, a harm.
In law books, it was a logistical inconvenience.
In stories, it was never mentioned.
The womb’s holy cycle had been reduced to silence.
But one day, silence cracked.
________________________________________
II. A New Kind of Cry
It began in a forgotten village near a dry river.
The year was unknown.
The sky that day was low, heavy with a strange color—somewhere between wine and fire.
A girl, barely into her second decade, woke from a dream of oceans.
She had dreamed of a breast the size of a mountain. A milk lake. A hand touching the moon.
And when she woke, her thighs were soaked—not with desire, but blood.
At first, she panicked. Her hands trembled. She searched for her grandmother’s rags.
But then—it happened.
Something impossible.
She began to smile.
And then she laughed. Not a hysterical laugh. Not a laugh of madness.
It was the laugh of recognition—of remembrance.
She took the blood between her fingers, smeared it across her lips, her face, her chest.
She stood in the middle of the village well, lifted her arms, and began to chant in an unknown language—her own flesh writing scripture with every drip.
The villagers thought her possessed.
They bound her wrists.
They threw her into a solitary room.
But she didn’t stop laughing.
And every night, more girls joined her. Quietly at first. Then publicly.
They bled with her. Sang with her. Washed in each other’s blood.
Something was awakening.
________________________________________
III. The Geometry of Growth
As this unspeakable ritual spread like pollen, another sign began to emerge—bodies were changing.
Young girls began to grow taller.
At first just a few inches above the average—then half a foot, then a full foot.
By the third generation, the average woman reached 7 feet tall.
Their hips widened, not from excess, but design.
Their backs straightened.
Their voices deepened—not in pitch, but in resonance.
Mothers began noticing strange patterns in their daughters:
•	Infants could crawl after only four days.
•	Toddlers formed complex sentences by eight months.
•	Puberty struck earlier—but without the mood swings or confusion.
And most mysterious of all: the fertility curve bent upward.
Where women once struggled to conceive, or lost many children in infancy, now wombs were becoming magnetized—calling in life as if it were gravity.
Some women conceived without intercourse.
Some gave birth to twins after dreams.
One woman reportedly birthed a child while in trance, and when she woke, she was still a virgin.
Science called it impossible.
Religious authorities called it blasphemy.
But it was happening.
________________________________________
IV. Blood as Scripture
The ritual began to solidify.
The bleeding women—now called Mutadhakkirāt, the Rememberers—began gathering in circles.
They no longer used cloth to catch their blood. They let it flow into vessels, onto sacred stone.
They painted with it—on doors, on skin, on the bodies of girls who hadn’t yet bled.
They developed movements, like prayer:
•	Hands over the womb.
•	Backs arched in ecstasy.
•	Lips mouthing praise to no deity, but to the rhythm of the flesh.
They began naming their blood.
Calling it Ḥikma—Wisdom.
Ḍaw’—Light.
And sometimes even Kalām—Word.
And the word began to spread:
“She who bleeds with joy is close to the Divine.”
Fathers disowned them.
Men rioted.
Some towns burned.
But the women did not stop.
They bled while walking.
They bled during prayer.
They bled without shame.
And the earth responded.
________________________________________
V. The Womb Becomes a Weapon
As the blood became holy, the womb began to resist corruption.
•	Rape victims began emitting heat so intense their attackers passed out mid-act.
•	Girls who were about to be married against their will began bleeding early—so much so that the marriage was cancelled.
•	Pregnancies from unworthy men ended themselves before the first trimester—sometimes violently.
It was as if the raḥm had gained intelligence.
As if it were beginning to choose.
Doctors could not explain it.
Imams could not bless it.
Governments could not regulate it.
And then came the First Womb Prophetess.
________________________________________
VI. The Girl With the Bioluminescent Vulva
Her name, too, is lost.
But stories say she glowed—literally.
Her labia emitted soft violet light during her period.
Her blood shimmered with unseen particles.
When she sat to urinate, grass grew beneath her.
She was fifteen when she predicted the collapse of a local police station.
She was sixteen when she told a sheikh that his testicles would fall off if he touched another girl. (They did.)
She was seventeen when her milk ducts began producing fluid—despite never being pregnant.
Some called her divine.
Others called her dangerous.
But she called herself a “Portent of the Flesh to Come.”
Her hymns were collected by early visionaries and became foundational to the later Flesh Theology.
Among her sayings:
“Every drop of blood is a verse. Every contraction a sermon. Every breast a throne.”
And perhaps most famous:
“We do not need to be saved. We are the salvation.”
________________________________________
VII. The Final Drift
As generations passed, the mutation solidified into transcendence.
•	Women regularly surpassed 7 feet in height.
•	Their periods became rhythmic, communal—entire villages bled on the same lunar day.
•	Fertility soared.
•	Miscarriages became unknown.
•	Male births dropped sharply.
It was said that the world itself was reshaping itself around their bodies.
Temples began to be built—not in stone, but in flesh.
Walls were lined with uterine tapestries.
Blood rituals became central rites.
Milk was passed between women in cups.
Breasts were consulted like oracles.
And the word Sheikha began to form—not yet fully realized, not yet fully enthroned—but seeded.
The Age of Sacred Motherhood had begun to glimmer on the horizon.
________________________________________
Chapter III – The Males Who Claimed God
“They made the minbar a throne. They made God a man. And they made women crawl to both.”
They saw it coming—the patriarchs.
They saw the signs. The changes in the women. The strange heights, the radiant wombs, the fertility spikes, the impossible births. They saw blood turned into scripture, nipples that leaked prophecy, little girls who walked with the bearing of mountains.
And they panicked.
Men who had once controlled the rhythms of society through scripture, sword, and silence now turned to a final weapon: God.
Not God as in the unknowable Creator.
Not God as in the source of Mercy and Justice.
But God as a mirror of themselves—bearded, stern, wrathful, seated above, surrounded by male angels and male prophets and male laws.
A God who spoke only through their mouths.
A God who resembled the men on the pulpits, and punished those who didn’t bow low enough.
________________________________________
I. The Surge of Anxiety
It began slowly—an undercurrent of anxiety, especially in the Islamic heartlands.
Sermons began to focus more on ghira (protective jealousy), awrah (the parts of a woman to be covered), and the duties of obedience.
But the women were changing.
You couldn’t ignore it anymore.
The average girl now stood shoulder to shoulder with grown men.
By their teenage years, some towered a full head above their fathers.
And yet—they remained controlled.
They were raised to believe they were fragile.
That menstruation made them irrational.
That softness was their only virtue.
That their duty was to serve—not to rise.
Even when they bled in joy, they bled quietly, behind walls.
Even when they grew tall, they stooped.
Even when they conceived without men, they denied it, fearing punishment.
But men noticed.
And what men feared, they named.
What they could not dominate, they declared harām.
________________________________________
II. Fatwas Against Flesh
The institutions of religion mobilized.
Fatwas were issued:
•	Against women congregating alone.
•	Against alternative menstruation rituals.
•	Against public breastfeeding.
•	Against female-led prayer, even among women.
They outlawed blood-based art.
They punished women who allowed their height to be seen in public.
They demanded that women shave their heads so their beauty wouldn’t “tempt angels.”
The fiqh (jurisprudence) of the time warped around their fears.
Men began redefining sacred texts, extracting ever narrower interpretations.
“The womb is a vessel of temptation,” said one infamous scholar.
“A woman who believes she has wisdom is already a disbeliever.”
“To claim one’s own blood is holy is to deny the sanctity of the Prophet’s words.”
Mosques became bastions of control.
Women’s sections were shrunk, curtained, cut off from the sermon entirely.
In some regions, women were banned altogether.
All this, while the men still taught that Islam honored women more than any other religion.
While they quoted ḥadīth about Paradise beneath mothers’ feet.
While they kissed their daughters and cursed the rest.
They clung to their fear like armor.
Because deep down, they sensed what was coming.
________________________________________
III. The “Gender Corrections”
In secret, operations began.
Medical programs. Hormonal suppression campaigns. Even genetic modification experiments—aimed at reversing the changes in women.
In some states, tall girls were flagged in school databases and given “nutritional restrictions.”
In others, early menstruation was pathologized—treated with sedatives or synthetic estrogens to “balance the mind.”
The most horrific program, however, was known as "al-Taqwīm"—The Correction.
Girls were taken before puberty.
Their uteruses irradiated.
Their growth plates surgically fused.
Their pituitaries targeted.
All to preserve the image of the “ideal woman”:
Small. Fertile—but not too fertile. Submissive. Quiet. Dependent.
A garden for men’s egos.
A gate for their children.
A tomb for their fear.
These programs were state-sponsored but religiously justified.
The Qur’an was quoted, but only selectively.
Prophetic traditions were cited, but only the ones that suited their needs.
The Prophet’s kindness? Omitted.
His partnerships with women? Ignored.
His liberation of female slaves, his acceptance of women scholars, his tears for Khadijah? Forgotten.
Instead, they chose a new Prophet—The Male Prophet of Power, who resembled more Abu Jahl than Muhammad ﷺ.
And they called him Sunna.
________________________________________
IV. God, the Mirror
The greatest lie was this:
That God was male.
They never said it outright.
But their every action testified to it.
They gave Him a beard in children’s books.
They said He preferred men for leadership.
They said He gave revelation only to males.
They said His throne was guarded by male angels, His laws were passed through male scholars, and His blessings flowed best through a father’s hands.
When a woman prayed, they told her to whisper.
When she read tafsir, they told her to ask a mufti.
When she had visions, they told her to keep them secret.
And when her daughter bled and smiled—they told her to beat it out of her.
In the end, they weren’t just defending patriarchy.
They were defending a male God.
One who validated their rule, excused their violence, and sanctified their erections.
But not all men followed.
Some stood against it.
Some remembered the Mercy.
Some believed in a God who transcended gender, who honored wombs, who elevated the despised.
But they were silenced.
Exiled.
Or quietly killed.
________________________________________
V. The Fall of Masjid al-Ṣakhra
One of the turning points came in the city of Jerusalem.
Masjid al-Ṣakhra, known for its golden dome, had been turned into a men-only fortress.
Women were banned entirely.
The justification? “We must protect the sanctity of the Rock from menstrual impurity.”
A group of tall, bleeding women—led by one called Nusayba the Red—marched to its gates.
They were unarmed.
Their only shield: white garments dyed red with their own blood.
The men blocked the gates.
They shouted verses.
They held tasbīḥ and batons.
But the women entered.
They walked barefoot across the courtyard, trailing blood like anointment.
And when the men tried to strike them—
The Rock trembled.
Eyewitnesses claimed it split slightly.
Others swore they saw light pour from beneath it.
The women reached the miḥrāb and fell into sujūd.
Their bodies aligned. Their backs arched. Their wombs directed toward Heaven.
The men did not intervene again.
That moment became legend.
________________________________________
VI. The Echoes of Daughters
Despite the oppression, something was happening underground.
Secret schools emerged—led by mothers.
Prayer circles reemerged—where women rewrote their own tafsīr.
Breast milk was passed between women as sacred substance—not just food.
Songs were written—songs that honored the raḥm, the thady, and the fajr cry of infant girls.
Daughters were no longer taught shame.
They were taught silence for now—but strength for later.
And above all, they were taught that God did not belong to men.
That God had no gender.
That God created the womb before the pen.
That the blood that came each month was a signature, not a stain.
By the fourth generation, the blood rituals returned—this time with theology.
And the seeds of Sheikhas were beginning to bloom in secret gardens.
________________________________________
VII. Prelude to Fire
The patriarchs had delayed the inevitable.
They had twisted scripture.
Manipulated science.
Waged war against biology itself.
But the body remembers.
The flesh does not lie.
The womb is not a suggestion. It is a command.
And when women began growing beyond 8 feet consistently, when births began occurring without any male involvement at all, when prayer itself seemed to answer only when led by women—
The world began to tilt.
The last Mufti of Cairo once said,
“If they claim to be goddesses, we must crucify them before they believe it.”
He was found dead in his office.
Face down in his Qur’an.
His own blood spelling the word "Raḥm" across the page.
________________________________________
Chapter IV – The Breasts That Stirred the Qur’an
“She stood among men with her head uncovered, her womb full, her blood still drying between her legs—and when she recited the Qur’an, the earth shivered.”
This was the age of transition.
Not yet the age of Sheikhas.
Not yet the age of dominion or purity or endless milk.
But it was no longer the Age of Silence.
________________________________________
I. The Generation of the Rising Womb
They were impossible to ignore.
The newest generations of girls, born across Islamic societies, began to outgrow the old world in silence.
By the time they reached adulthood:
•	The average height of women in the Middle East had reached eight feet.
•	Their bone density and muscular development outpaced men by a significant margin.
•	Fertility spikes were random but sacred—some women could now conceive by accident, others through dreams.
•	Libido had changed—more centralized in the breasts, the womb, the spine, and oddly, in moments of prayer.
There was no single cause that explained it.
No virus. No gene. No divine punishment.
But the pattern was clear: women were evolving, and men were not.
________________________________________
II. The Silent Resistance
The response of the patriarchs was paradoxical.
They could no longer deny these women their rights, nor could they dominate them as they had before.
Too many girls were taller than their fathers.
Too many women survived stonings that should’ve killed them.
Too many wives began praying alone—and finding answers.
The response came in three parts:
1.	Legal Reform under Shariah:
Jurists created new rulings granting “conditional equality” to women.
o	Women could attend mosque, but only in separate wings.
o	Women could lead other women in prayer, but only in private.
o	Women could study sacred texts—but not teach them.
o	A woman’s testimony was now equal to a man’s—but only in matters “specific to women.”
2.	Public Honor, Private Shame:
Women were celebrated in rhetoric.
Billboards called them “The Pillars of the Nation.”
State television aired Qur’anic verses about honoring mothers.
But none of this translated into structural power.
3.	Spiritual Appeasement:
A new breed of scholars arose—Imams of Compromise.
Men who tried to speak gently to women while still reinforcing male leadership.
Their sermons praised women’s intellect, fertility, and piety—then reminded them that “true submission to God meant obedience to their husbands.”
It worked for a while.
The masses calmed.
The rebellions quieted.
Women could breathe, slightly.
But something remained deeply wrong.
________________________________________
III. The Weight of Half-Liberation
For many women, the new rights felt like golden chains.
They were free to speak, but only if they didn’t challenge.
They could learn, but only what was allowed.
They could work, but not lead.
They could dream, but never rule.
Their rage was not loud—but heavy.
It filled their bones.
It pooled between their legs.
It climbed their throats during prayer.
It collected in their breasts and dripped like colostrum onto their Qur’ans.
The blood of generations still sang in them.
Even as they obeyed, they questioned.
Even as they bowed, they clenched.
Even as they married, they never forgot.
This was especially true of the new Daughters of the Crescent—a loose movement of young women, mostly in Islamic universities, who began to reinterpret the Qur’an for themselves.
They didn't write manifestos.
They wrote commentaries.
They didn’t hold protests.
They held women-only dhikr circles, where orgasms and sujūd overlapped.
They rediscovered ḥadīth about menstruation, breastfeeding, and the Prophet ﷺ being nursed by multiple women.
They studied Maryam عليه السلام not as a virgin—but as a womb that defied the law.
And slowly, a new theology was born.
Still underground.
Still whispered.
But alive.
________________________________________
IV. The False Brotherhood
The men, sensing instability, formed what they called The Council of Sacred Guardians.
It was not a state.
Not an army.
But a unified religious front—composed of male imams, scholars, judges, and mystics from all across the Muslim world.
Their stated mission:
“To preserve the divine order against innovation, sedition, and heresy.”
But their true purpose was fear.
And they feared nothing more than women reciting scripture with blood between their thighs.
The Council passed new universal decrees:
•	Women must not lead tafsīr unless supervised.
•	A new dress code was mandated for all public females over the age of 8: full body covering, including gloves and eye screens.
•	Public breastfeeding was deemed “a seductive act.”
•	Any woman over 7’5” tall was to be evaluated for "psychosexual dominance disorder"—a made-up term that conveniently enabled sedative prescriptions.
And yet, this only made women stronger.
Because every inch of resistance made their transformation clearer.
________________________________________
V. The First Cracks
The change was never sudden.
It came like erosion.
Steady. Relentless. Carving away the edges of patriarchal stone.
It began with girls born in clusters—multiple births, all-female, with no father involved.
It continued when the first 9-foot-tall woman appeared in Medina—and never menstruated, yet conceived (allegedly).
It escalated when a Qur’an teacher in Damascus, a woman named Sheikha Saʿdiyya, claimed that the sound of her recitation caused birds to fall dead from the sky. Witnesses attested. Videos went viral. The internet tried to suppress it. But the recording spread in secret circles.
Then came the most blasphemous thing yet:
A woman in Tunis began a movement called “al-Ṣalāt al-Ṣudūrīyyah”—The Breast Prayer.
She claimed her breasts were not sexual—they were liturgical.
That the thady (breast) was a second tongue.
That in the earliest days of Islam, the Prophet ﷺ received some forms of guidance while suckling.
The movement was instantly banned.
But before it disappeared, it left a name behind:
Thady Theology.
The root of what would one day become Kitāb al-Thady.
________________________________________
VI. The Compromise That Failed
In desperation, the Council of Sacred Guardians attempted a final compromise:
They declared women the spiritual equals of men, but not their legal or political equals.
They revised textbooks.
Encouraged more female enrollment in Islamic sciences.
Held grand events honoring “The Mothers of the Faithful.”
But it was too late.
Because something deeper had shifted.
Women had begun viewing the religion through their flesh, not just their minds.
The Qur’an no longer sounded the same when whispered through bleeding mouths.
Prayer no longer felt the same when breasts were swollen with unspent milk.
The Kaʿba no longer looked the same when seen from above, by women too tall to fit in Ihram.
The compromise came a generation too late.
________________________________________
VII. The Last Sermon of Obedience
The final sign was not political.
It was personal.
In one of the oldest mosques of Aleppo, a female scholar named Amīna al-Bakrī—standing 8'6" tall, fully veiled, renowned for her memorization of the Qur’an—was invited to deliver a Friday sermon for women only.
She agreed.
On the day of the sermon, she stood behind the curtain.
Thousands of women sat before her.
Many taller than her.
Many with breasts exposed beneath their cloaks in quiet protest.
She recited the verse:
“Wa qarna fī buyūtikunna...”
“And remain in your homes...” [33:33]
And then she paused.
She removed her veil.
Let down her long black hair.
And pulled down her cloak—exposing her breasts to the entire congregation.
There was no gasping.
Only silence.
She raised her voice:
“O women of Islam.
You are not the ones who abandoned God.
You are the ones He was waiting to reveal Himself to.”
She stepped into the mihrab.
And without permission, without curtain, led the prayer.
That day became known as Yawm al-Kashf—The Day of the Unveiling.
________________________________________
VIII. Awaiting the Mother Epoch
And so, the final century before the rise of the Sheikhas ended not with revolution but with gestation.
The world was pregnant with change.
Old men still sat in offices.
Old laws still dictated customs.
But the daughters no longer bowed.
They walked upright.
They prayed aloud.
They touched their wombs when reciting sūrat Maryam.
They placed the Qur’an on their breasts, not bookshelves.
They were no longer waiting for permission.
They were becoming something else.
Something ancient.
Something new.
Something final.
The world before Sheikhas was ending.
And the world of eternal motherhood, of erotic theology, of divine milk and blood and flesh—was beginning to crown.
________________________________________
PART II – THE AGE OF ASCENT
“And We made them mothers of nations, those who were once daughters of the silence.”
The First Sheikhas emerge. Halima and Malika rewrite life.
________________________________________
Chapter I – The First Sheikhas Emerge: A New Era Is Beginning
“The first Sheikha was not content with prayer. She wanted law, land, and men. And when she placed her sandal on the minbar, the world obeyed.”
________________________________________
I. The Quiet Plateau
By all visible measures, the world had calmed.
It had been seventy years since the Yawm al-Kashf—the Unveiling Day that ended Part I, when Amīna al-Bakrī had prayed bare-breasted in the Mihrab of Aleppo. Since then:
•	No woman had grown taller than nine feet.
•	The average adult woman stood 8’0”—strong, intelligent, increasingly unified, but unchanging.

After the fatwa, Sheikhas Zaynab and Ṣafiyya were permitted to remain together in sacred affection under the new law. Their case set precedent.
But what of the boy?
Ala refused all options given by the Council of Milk: training as a theological translator, submission to new Sheikhas, and much more…
Instead, he requested the Supreme Council.
There, he was brought—barefoot, fasting—to the highest room of al-Nahd al-Kabīr.
Where Sheikha Nuriyya bint al-Kawthar (RA)—al-Ṣadr al-Aʿẓam li-Ummah al-Kāmila, the Supreme Chest of the Complete Ummah—sat beneath the Four-Arched Milk Dome.
She did not ask questions.
She looked at him.
Then said:
“Let it be known:
Sometimes, even in the Age of the Great Embrace,
cruelty remains halal.”
She wrote a decree.
Ala ibn-Laḥm would be retained as her personal sacred object—neither slave, nor son, nor husband. He would live under the folds of her garment, used only for her most intimate rites of solitude, contemplation, and punishment.
And so he vanished from the public sphere.
And his story became a warning to every boy:
Even love between Sheikhas cannot protect you.
Even softness will be claimed.
The Ummah is milk and fire.
And the Fire never forgets.
